title: visStatistics: The right test, visualised
text: general linear model, response \(Y\) modelled linear combination \(k-1\) predictors \(x_j\). general linear model observation \(,\;= 1, \ldots, N\) \[\begin{equation} (\#eq:glm) Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{i1} + \cdots +       \beta_{k-1} x_{,k-1} + \varepsilon_i, \end{equation}\] \(Y_i\) response observation \(\), \(x_{ij}\) value predictor \(j\) observation \(\), \(\beta_0, \beta_1, \ldots, \beta_{k-1}\) \(k\) parameters, \(\varepsilon_i\) model error term. model error terms \(\varepsilon_i\) assumed independent normally distributed expectation 0 constant variance \(\sigma^2\), short \[\varepsilon_i \sim \mathscr{N}(0, \sigma^2), \quad\mathrm{mutually\; independent} \] Eq.  @ref(eq:glm), special cases used visstat() follow predictor structure: Student’s t-test uses one binary indicator variable \(x_{i1}\), \(x_{i1}=0\) group 1 \(x_{i1}=1\) group 2. Let \(\mu_1\) \(\mu_2\) denote expected values two population groups. expected values response, obtain \[E(Y_i \mid x_{i1}=0)=\mu_1=\beta_0\] group 1 \[E(Y_i \mid x_{i1}=1)=\mu_2=\beta_0+\beta_1\] group 2. Testing \(H_0: \beta_1 = 0\) therefore equivalent testing \(H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2\). Fisher’s ANOVA generalises coding \(k-1\) binary indicator variables \(k\) groups; testing \(H_0: \beta_1 = \cdots = \beta_{k-1} = 0\) equivalent testing equality group means. Simple linear regression uses one continuous predictor; \(H_0: \beta_1 = 0\) examines whether linear relationship exists. model error terms \(\varepsilon_i\) Eq.  @ref(eq:glm) observed. observable counterpart residuals. fitting corresponding linear model, residual \[\begin{equation} e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i, (\#eq:raw-residual) \end{equation}\] \(y_i\) observed value \(\hat{y}_i\) fitted value observation \(\).

title: visStatistics: The right test, visualised
text: Shapiro–Wilk test evaluates whether sample \(x_1,\ldots,x_n\) comes normal distribution. Let \(x_{(1)}\le \cdots \le x_{(n)}\) order statistics. Introduce reference sample \(Z_1,\ldots,Z_n\) independent standard normal random variables, .e. \(Z_i \sim N(0,1)\) \(\), let \(Z_{(1)}\le \cdots \le Z_{(n)}\) order statistics used construct Shapiro–Wilk weights. Let \(m_i = \operatorname{E}(Z_{()})\) \(v_{ij} = \operatorname{Cov}(Z_{()}, Z_{(j)})\) \(,j = 1,\ldots,n\). Define \(\mathbf{m} = (m_1,\ldots,m_n)^\top\) \(V = (v_{ij})_{,j=1}^n\). vector \(\mathbf{m}\) contains expected standard-normal order statistics, \(V\) covariance matrix. Let \(\mathbf{}=(a_1,\ldots,a_n)^\top\) resulting vector normalised weights ordered observed sample values \[\mathbf{} =\frac{V^{-1}\mathbf{m}} {\sqrt{\left(\mathbf{m}^\top V^{-1}V^{-1}\mathbf{m}\right)}}.\] Shapiro–Wilk statistic (Shapiro Wilk 1965) \[\begin{equation} W=\frac{\left(\sum_{=1}^{n} a_i x_{()}\right)^2} {\sum_{=1}^{n} (x_i-\bar{x})^2} (\#eq:shapiro-w) \end{equation}\] \(W\) takes values \((0, 1]\); values close 1 indicate normality.

title: visStatistics: The right test, visualised
text: Anderson–Darling test (Anderson Darling 1952) particularly sensitive deviations tails distribution (Razali Wah 2011; Yap Sim 2011). Let \(z_i = (x_{()} - \bar{x})/s,\; =1,2,\ldots,n\) standardised order statistics \(x_i\), \(s\) sample standard deviation, let \(\Phi\) denote standard normal cumulative distribution function. test statistic \[\begin{equation} ^2 = -n - \frac{1}{n}\sum_{=1}^{n}(2i-1)         \left[\ln\Phi(z_i) + \ln\!\left(1 - \Phi(z_{n+1-})\right)\right] (\#eq:anderson-a2) \end{equation}\] implementation uses ad.test() nortest (Gross Ligges 2015).

title: visStatistics: The right test, visualised
text: package implementation uses Levene’s original mean-centred proposal (Levene 1960). Levene test statistic one-way ANOVA \(F\) statistic, computed absolute residuals \(|e_{ij}|\) place responses \(x_{ij}\); corresponding Fisher ANOVA formula given Eq. @ref(eq:fisher-f): \[\begin{equation} F_L = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{=1}^{k} n_i (\overline{|e|}_i - \overline{|e|})^2\;/\;(k-1)}          {\displaystyle\sum_{=1}^{k}\sum_{j=1}^{n_i}(|e_{ij}| - \overline{|e|}_i)^2\;/\;(N-k)}, (\#eq:levene-f) \end{equation}\] \(\overline{|e|}_i\) within-group mean absolute residuals \(\overline{|e|}\) overall mean.

title: visStatistics: The right test, visualised
text: Bartlett’s test statistic (Bartlett 1937) \[\begin{equation} K^2 = \frac{(N-k)\ln s_p^2 - \displaystyle\sum_{=1}^k (n_i-1)\ln s_i^2} {1 + \dfrac{1}{3(k-1)}\!\left(\displaystyle\sum_{=1}^k \frac{1}{n_i-1} - \frac{1}{N-k}\right)}, (\#eq:bartlett-k2) \end{equation}\] \(k\) number groups, \(N = \sum_{=1}^k n_i\) total sample size, \(n_i\) sample size group \(\), \(s_i^2\) sample variance group \(\), \(s_p^2\) pooled variance \[s_p^2 = \frac{1}{N-k}\sum_{=1}^k (n_i-1)\,s_i^2.\] null hypothesis statistic approximately follows \(\chi^2(k-1)\).

title: visStatistics: The right test, visualised
text: simple linear regression, group-based variance tests applicable. package implementation bp.test() performs Koenker variant (Koenker 1981) Breusch–Pagan test (Breusch Pagan 1979), tests whether \(N\) squared residuals \(e_i^2\) vary systematically fitted values regression model \(\hat{y}_i\). Breusch–Pagan statistic defined : \[\begin{equation} BP = N R^2_\text{aux} (\#eq:breusch-pagan-bp), \end{equation}\] \(R^2_\text{aux}\) denotes coefficient determination regressing \(e_i^2\) \(\hat{y}_i\): \[R^2_\text{aux} = 1 - \frac{\sum_{=1}^{N} (e_i^2 - \widehat{e_i^2})^2}      {\sum_{=1}^{N} (e_i^2 - \overline{e^2})^2}.\] \(\widehat{e_i^2}\) fitted values auxiliary regression \(\overline{e^2}\) mean squared residuals. null hypothesis homoscedasticity, \(BP\) compared asymptotically \(\chi^2(k-1)\) distribution.

text: Fisher’s one-way ANOVA generalises mean comparison two groups tests null hypothesis means \(k\) groups equal. Fisher’s ANOVA test statistic \[\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} F &= \frac{MS_\text{}}{MS_\text{within}}    = \frac{SS_\text{}/(k-1)}{SS_\text{within}/(N-k)}    = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{=1}^{k} n_i           (\bar{x}_i - \bar{x})^2\;/\;(k-1)}           {\displaystyle\sum_{=1}^{k}\sum_{j=1}^{n_i}           (x_{ij}-\bar{x}_i)^2\;/\;(N-k)} \end{aligned}, (\#eq:fisher-f) \end{equation}\] \(MS_\text{}\) \(MS_\text{within}\) -group within-group mean squares, \(SS_\text{}\) \(SS_\text{within}\) corresponding sums squares, \(k\) number groups, \(N = \sum_{=1}^k n_i\) total sample size, \(\bar{x}_i\) mean group \(\), \(\bar{x}\) overall mean, \(x_{ij}\) observation \(j\) group \(\). Eq. @ref(eq:fisher-f) follows two-sample case (\(k=2\)), squared test statistic Student’s t-test equals Fisher ANOVA test statistic, \(t^2 = F\), resulting identical \(p\)-values t.test(var.equal = TRUE) aov(). \(H_0: \mu_1 = \cdots = \mu_k\), statistic follows \(F(k-1, N-k)\).

title: visStatistics: The right test, visualised
text: Pearson’s \(\chi^2\) test evaluates null hypothesis two categorical variables independent. Let \(O_{ij}\) \(E_{ij}\) denote observed expected frequencies row \(\) column \(j\) \(R \times C\) contingency table, rows index \(R\) levels response \(y\) columns \(C\) levels predictor \(x\). Pearson residual cell \((,j)\) \[\begin{equation} r_{ij} = \frac{O_{ij} - E_{ij}}{\sqrt{E_{ij}}}, \quad = 1,\ldots,R,\quad j = 1,\ldots,C. (\#eq:pearson-residual) \end{equation}\]  test statistic Pearson’s \(\chi^2\) test \[\begin{equation} \chi^2 = \sum_{=1}^{R}\sum_{j=1}^{C} r_{ij}^2, (\#eq:pearson-chi) \end{equation}\] null hypothesis independence, statistic compared \(\chi^2\) distribution \((R-1)(C-1)\) degrees freedom. \(2\times 2\) tables, Yates’ continuity correction applied default.

title: visStatistics: The right test, visualised
text: Fisher’s exact test (fisher.test()) applied Cochran’s rule (Cochran 1954) violated. test calculates exact \(p\)-value conditioning observed margins \(R \times C\) contingency table null hypothesis independence. Let \(T = (n_{ij})\) denote observed table. maintain consistency y ~ x (response ~ predictor) framework used throughout visStatistics, rows (\(=1,\ldots,R\)) represent levels response variable \(y\) columns (\(j=1,\ldots,C\)) represent levels predictor \(x\). row totals \(n_{\cdot} = \sum_{j=1}^C n_{ij}\) column totals \(n_{\cdot j} = \sum_{=1}^R n_{ij}\). \(2 \times 2\) case (\(R=2, C=2\)), table structured follows: \[\begin{array}{c|cc|c} & x_1 & x_2 & \text{Row sums} \\ \hline y_1 & n_{11} & n_{12} & n_{1\cdot} \\ y_2 & n_{21} & n_{22} & n_{2\cdot} \\ \hline \text{Column sums} & n_{\cdot 1} & n_{\cdot 2} & N \end{array}\] exact probability observing table null hypothesis independence, given fixed margins, given hypergeometric probability mass function: \[\begin{equation} \mathbb{P}(T \mid n_{1\cdot}, n_{2\cdot}, n_{\cdot 1}, n_{\cdot 2}) = \frac{\binom{n_{1\cdot}}{n_{11}}         \binom{n_{2\cdot}}{n_{21}}}{\binom{N}{n_{\cdot 1}}}, (\#eq:fisher-exact) \end{equation}\] \(N = n_{1\cdot} + n_{2\cdot} = n_{\cdot 1} + n_{\cdot 2}\) total sample size. \(p\)-value computed summing probabilities tables margins whose probabilities null less equal observed table. general \(R \times C\) tables, fisher.test() generalises approach using multivariate hypergeometric distribution. \(2 \times 2\) tables, fisher.test() additionally returns conditional maximum likelihood estimate odds ratio \[\begin{equation} \widehat{\mathrm{}} = n_{11}n_{22}/(n_{12}n_{21}) (\#eq:odds-ratio) \end{equation}\] confidence interval.

title: visStatistics: The right test, visualised
text: Kendall’s \(\tau_b\) tests null hypothesis monotone association two ordered variables. two ordinal variables \(n\) joint observations, let \(C\) denote number concordant pairs (whose ranks agree variables) \(D\) number discordant pairs. Kendall’s \(\tau_b\) defined \[\begin{equation} \tau_b \;=\; \frac{C - D} {\sqrt{\left(n_0 - n_1\right)\left(n_0 - n_2\right)}}, (\#eq:kendall-tau-b) \end{equation}\] \(n_0 = n(n-1)/2\) total number observation pairs, \(n_1 = \sum_i t_i(t_i-1)/2\) number pairs tied response, \(n_2 = \sum_j u_j(u_j-1)/2\) number pairs tied predictor. denominator correction makes \(\tau_b\) attain \(\pm 1\) even ties, Spearman’s \(\rho\) (Kendall 1945). ordered levels (e.g., five-point Likert items), ties unavoidable; principal reason prefer \(\tau_b\) Spearman’s \(\rho\) setting (Agresti 2010). visstat() calls cor.test(.numeric(y), .numeric(x), method = "kendall", exact = FALSE) reports \(\tau_b\), asymptotic test statistic \(z = \tau_b / \operatorname{SE}(\tau_b)\), two-sided \(p\)-value.

title: visStatistics: The right test, visualised
text: two numeric variables correlation = TRUE, visstat() calls cor.test(x, y, method = "spearman") test monotone association \(x\) \(y\) using ranks. Spearman’s \(\rho\) Pearson’s \(r\) applied ranks: \[\begin{equation} \rho = r(\operatorname{rank}(x), \operatorname{rank}(y)), (\#eq:spearman-rho) \end{equation}\] \(r(u, v)\) denotes Pearson’s correlation coefficient: \[r(u,v) = \frac{\sum_{=1}^{n}(u_i-\bar u)(v_i-\bar v)} {\sqrt{\sum_{=1}^{n}(u_i-\bar u)^2}\, \sqrt{\sum_{=1}^{n}(v_i-\bar v)^2}}.\] \(u_i = \operatorname{rank}(x_i)\) \(v_i = \operatorname{rank}(y_i)\) ranks \(n\) paired observations, \(\bar{u}\) \(\bar{v}\) sample means. inference, cor.test(..., method = "spearman") computes exact \(p\)-value small samples without ties evaluating \(n!\) rank permutations. larger samples ties present, uses approximation null distribution rank association measure asymptotic transformation. distributional assumptions original data required. separate Pearson-correlation branch implemented. simple linear regression intercept, two-sided test zero slope two-sided test zero Pearson correlation return \(p\)-value. Pearson correlation therefore add separate inferential route default regression branch.

title: Breusch-Pagan Test for Heteroscedasticity — bp.test
text: object class "htest" components: statistic value chi-squared test statistic. parameter degrees freedom. p.value p-value test. method character string indicating test performed. data.name character string giving name model.

title: Breusch-Pagan Test for Heteroscedasticity — bp.test
text: Implements Koenker variant Breusch-Pagan test, regresses squared raw residuals fitted values. test statistic : $$BP = n \cdot R^2$$ : \(n\) = sample size \(R^2\) = coefficient determination auxiliary regression         \(e_i^2\) \(\hat{y}_i\) null hypothesis homoscedasticity, test statistic follows chi-squared distribution: \(BP \sim \chi^2(k-1)\) \(k\) number parameters model (including intercept). Large values test statistic (small p-values) provide evidence homoscedasticity.

title: Breusch-Pagan Test for Heteroscedasticity — bp.test
text: Breusch, T. S., Pagan, . R. (1979). simple test heteroscedasticity random coefficient variation. Econometrica, 47(5), 1287-1294. DOI: 10.2307/1911963 Koenker, R. (1981). note studentizing test heteroscedasticity. Journal Econometrics, 17(1), 107-112. DOI: 10.1016/0304-4076(81)90062-2

title: Breusch-Pagan Test for Heteroscedasticity — bp.test
code: # Example with homoscedastic errors set.seed(123) x <- runif(100) y <- 2 + 3*x + rnorm(100, sd = 1) model1 <- lm(y ~ x) bp.test(model1)  # Should not reject (p > 0.05) #>  #> 	Breusch-Pagan test for heteroscedasticity #>  #> data:  model1 #> BP = 0.025212, df = 1, p-value = 0.8738 #>   # Example with heteroscedastic errors (variance increases with x) set.seed(456) x <- runif(100) y <- 2 + 3 *x + rnorm(100, sd = 0.5 + 2*x) model2 <- lm(y ~ x) bp.test(model2)  # Should reject (p < 0.05) #>  #> 	Breusch-Pagan test for heteroscedasticity #>  #> data:  model2 #> BP = 20.435, df = 1, p-value = 6.169e-06 #>

code: x <- ToothGrowth$supp y <- ToothGrowth$len tt <- list("t-test-statistics" = t.test(y ~ x, var.equal = TRUE)) effect_size(tt, x = x, y = y) #> $name #> [1] "Hedges' g" #>  #> $estimate #> [1] 0.4880931 #>  #> $effect_size_method #> [1] "Hedges' g using pooled standard deviation" #>   kw <- list(   "Kruskal Wallis rank sum test" = kruskal.test(Petal.Width ~ Species,                                                data = iris) ) effect_size(kw, x = iris$Species, y = iris$Petal.Width) #> $name #> [1] "eta-squared based on H" #>  #> $estimate #> [1] 0.8788121 #>  #> $effect_size_method #> [1] "Eta-squared based on H for Kruskal-Wallis rank sum test" #>   tab <- matrix(c(10, 5, 4, 12), nrow = 2) effect_size(chisq.test(tab)) #> $name #> [1] "phi" #>  #> $estimate #> [1] 0.3535596 #>  #> $effect_size_method #> [1] "Phi coefficient for 2 x 2 contingency table" #>   if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ ## Large-sample example with a statistically significant Student's ## t-test p-value but a small effect size, measured by Hedges' g ## using the pooled standard deviation. A small mean shift is added ## to noisy normal data. Because N is large, the t-test p-value ## becomes small, while Hedges' g remains close to zero. ## The residual Shapiro-Wilk p-value in the diagnostic panel is NA ## because shapiro.test() is limited to n <= 5000. set.seed(20260525) n <- 2501 mean_shift <- 0.1 group <- factor(rep(c("control", "treatment"), each = n)) response <- rnorm(2 * n) + rep(c(0, mean_shift), each = n) res <- visstat(group, response) res[["t-test-statistics"]]$method res[["t-test-statistics"]]$p.value res$effect_size$effect_size_method res$effect_size$estimate } # }

title: Mean-centred Levene Test for Homogeneity of Variance — levene.test
text: observation \(y_{ij}\) group \(\), compute absolute deviation group mean: $$z_{ij} = |y_{ij} - \bar{y}_i|$$ \(\bar{y}_i\) mean group \(\). test statistic F-statistic one-way ANOVA \(z_{ij}\) values: $$F = \frac{(N-k) \sum_{=1}^{k} n_i (\bar{z}_i - \bar{z})^2}{(k-1) \sum_{=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (z_{ij} - \bar{z}_i)^2}$$ : \(k\) = number groups \(N\) = total sample size \(n_i\) = sample size group \(\) \(\bar{z}_i\) = mean absolute deviations group \(\) \(\bar{z}\) = overall mean absolute deviations null hypothesis equal variances, test statistic follows F-distribution: \(F \sim F(k-1, N-k)\).

title: Mean-centred Levene Test for Homogeneity of Variance — levene.test

title: Mean-centred Levene Test for Homogeneity of Variance — levene.test
code: set.seed(123) y <- c(rnorm(10), rnorm(10, sd = 2), rnorm(10, sd = 0.5)) g <- factor(rep(1:3, each = 10)) levene.test(y, g) #>  #> 	Mean-centred Levene Test #>  #> data:  absolute deviations from group means #> F = 4.3375, df1 = 2, df2 = 27, p-value = 0.02325 #>   # Usage with data frame df <- data.frame(response = y, group = g) levene.test(response, group, data = df) #>  #> 	Mean-centred Levene Test #>  #> data:  absolute deviations from group means #> F = 4.3375, df1 = 2, df2 = 27, p-value = 0.02325 #>   # Example with unequal variances (should reject null hypothesis) set.seed(456) y_unequal <- c(rnorm(15, sd = 1), rnorm(15, sd = 5), rnorm(15, sd = 0.2)) g_unequal <- factor(rep(c("A", "B", "C"), each = 15)) levene.test(y_unequal, g_unequal) #>  #> 	Mean-centred Levene Test #>  #> data:  absolute deviations from group means #> F = 35.937, df1 = 2, df2 = 42, p-value = 8.004e-10 #>

code: # Formula interface mtcars$am <- as.factor(mtcars$am) visstat(mpg ~ am, data = mtcars)    # Standardised usage visstat(mtcars$am, mtcars$mpg)    ## Student's t-test (equal variances, two groups) # When residuals are normally distributed and Levene's test indicates # homoscedasticity, the classic Student's t-test with pooled variance is used df <- droplevels(subset(PlantGrowth, group %in% c("ctrl", "trt1"))) visstat(df$group,df$weight)    ## Welch's t-test (unequal variances, two groups) # When residuals are normally distributed but Levene's test indicates # heteroscedasticity, Welch's t-test is used visstat(mtcars$am, mtcars$mpg)    ## Wilcoxon rank sum test (non-normal, two groups) # When residuals are not normally distributed grades_gender <- data.frame(   Sex = as.factor(c(rep("Girl", 20), rep("Boy", 20))),   Grade = c(     19.3, 18.1, 15.2, 18.3, 7.9, 6.2, 19.4, 20.3, 9.3, 11.3,     18.2, 17.5, 10.2, 20.1, 13.3, 17.2, 15.1, 16.2, 17.3, 16.5,     5.1, 15.3, 17.1, 14.8, 15.4, 14.4, 7.5, 15.5, 6.0, 17.4,     7.3, 14.3, 13.5, 8.0, 19.5, 13.4, 17.9, 17.7, 16.4, 15.6   ) ) visstat(grades_gender$Sex, grades_gender$Grade)    ## Fisher's ANOVA (equal variances, >2 groups) # When residuals are normally distributed and Levene's test indicates # homoscedasticity, classic Fisher's ANOVA with TukeyHSD post-hoc is used. # Different green letters indicate significant differences between groups.  visstat(PlantGrowth$group, PlantGrowth$weight)    ## Welch's one-way ANOVA (unequal variances, >2 groups) set.seed(123) values <- c(rnorm(20, 10, 1),rnorm(20, 15, 5),rnorm(20, 12, 2)) groups <- factor(rep(c("A", "B", "C"), each = 20)) visstat(groups, values)   ## Kruskal-Wallis (non-normal, >2 groups) # When residuals are not normally distributed, kruskal.test() is followed by  # pairwise.wilcox.test.  visstat(iris$Species, iris$Petal.Width)    ## Simple linear regression (both numeric) visstat(trees$Height, trees$Girth, conf.level = 0.99)    ## Pearson's Chi-squared test (both factors, large expected counts) HairEyeColorDataFrame <- counts_to_cases(as.data.frame(HairEyeColor)) visstat(HairEyeColorDataFrame$Eye, HairEyeColorDataFrame$Hair)    ## Fisher's exact test (both factors, small expected counts) HairEyeColorMaleFisher <- HairEyeColor[, , 1] blackBrownHazelGreen <- HairEyeColorMaleFisher[1:2, 3:4] blackBrownHazelGreen <- counts_to_cases(as.data.frame(blackBrownHazelGreen)) visstat(blackBrownHazelGreen$Eye, blackBrownHazelGreen$Hair)   ## Save PNG visstat(blackBrownHazelGreen$Hair, blackBrownHazelGreen$Eye,         graphicsoutput = "png", plotDirectory = tempdir())  ## Custom plot name visstat(iris$Species, iris$Petal.Width,         graphicsoutput = "pdf", plotName = "kruskal_iris", plotDirectory = tempdir()) #> Warning: calling par(new=TRUE) with no plot

title: Automated Visualization of Statistical Hypothesis Testing — visstat_core
text: decision logic selecting statistical test described . details, please refer package's vignette("visStatistics"). Throughout, data class numeric integer referred numeric, data class factor referred categorical. significance level alpha defined one minus confidence level, given argument conf.level. Assumptions normality homoscedasticity considered met corresponding test yields p-value greater alpha = 1 - conf.level. choice statistical tests performed visstat_core() depends whether data numeric categorical, number levels categorical variable, distribution data, chosen conf.level. function prioritises interpretable visual output tests remain valid assumptions, following logic : (1) response numerical predictor categorical, tests central tendencies performed. decision logic, please refer packages vignette vignette("visStatistics") (2): response predictor numerical, linear model lm() fitted, residual diagnostics confidence band plot. (3): variables categorical, visstat_core() uses chisq.test() fisher.test() depending expected counts, following Cochran's rule (Cochran (1954) <doi:10.2307/3001666>). Implemented main tests: t.test(), wilcox.test(), aov(), oneway.test(), lm(), kruskal.test(), fisher.test(), chisq.test(). Implemented tests assumptions: Normality: shapiro.test() ad.test() Heteroscedasticity: bartlett.test() levene.test() bp_test() Implemented post hoc tests: TukeyHSD() aov() games.howell  oneway.test() pairwise.wilcox.test() kruskal.test()

title: Automated Visualization of Statistical Hypothesis Testing — visstat_core
code: # Welch Two Sample t-test (t.test()) visstat_core(mtcars, "mpg", "am")   #> Warning: Statistical assumptions violated: #> Homoscedasticity violated (Breusch-Pagan p = 0.0242 ) #> Analysis proceeded but interpret results cautiously. #> RECOMMENDATION: Consider exploring alternatives outside visstat() such as data transformations, #> generalised linear models, or robust regression. For a non-causal alternative #> consider rerunning with correlation = TRUE.  ## Wilcoxon rank sum test (wilcox.test()) grades_gender <- data.frame(   Sex = as.factor(c(rep("Girl", 20), rep("Boy", 20))),   Grade = c(     19.3, 18.1, 15.2, 18.3, 7.9, 6.2, 19.4,     20.3, 9.3, 11.3, 18.2, 17.5, 10.2, 20.1, 13.3, 17.2, 15.1, 16.2, 17.3,     16.5, 5.1, 15.3, 17.1, 14.8, 15.4, 14.4, 7.5, 15.5, 6.0, 17.4,     7.3, 14.3, 13.5, 8.0, 19.5, 13.4, 17.9, 17.7, 16.4, 15.6   ) ) visstat_core(grades_gender, "Grade", "Sex")    ## Welch's oneway ANOVA not assuming equal variances (oneway.test()) anova_npk <- visstat_core(npk, "yield", "block")   anova_npk # prints summary of tests #> Object of class 'visstat' #>  #> Available components: #> [1] "summary statistics of ANOVA" "post-hoc analysis "          #> [3] "conf.level"                  "effect_size"                  ## Kruskal-Wallis rank sum test (kruskal.test()) visstat_core(iris, "Petal.Width", "Species")   visstat_core(InsectSprays, "count", "spray")    ## Simple linear regression  (lm()) visstat_core(trees, "Girth", "Height", conf.level = 0.99)    ## Pearson's Chi-squared test (chisq.test()) ### Transform array to data.frame HairEyeColorDataFrame <- counts_to_cases(as.data.frame(HairEyeColor)) visstat_core(HairEyeColorDataFrame, "Hair", "Eye")    ## Fisher's exact test (fisher.test()) HairEyeColorMaleFisher <- HairEyeColor[, , 1] ### slicing out a 2 x2 contingency table blackBrownHazelGreen <- HairEyeColorMaleFisher[1:2, 3:4] blackBrownHazelGreen <- counts_to_cases(as.data.frame(blackBrownHazelGreen)) fisher_stats <- visstat_core(blackBrownHazelGreen, "Hair", "Eye")

title: visStatistics 0.2.1
text: Reduced exported standalone functions. following routing plotting helpers longer exported documented standalone functions: vis_anova(), vis_numeric(), gh_letters() now internal helpers. Legacy normality helpers vis_group_normality() pooled_normality_test() archived longer shipped. vis_anova_assumptions() remains internal deprecated wrapper vis_lm_assumptions(), longer exported documented.

title: visStatistics 0.2.0
text: Unified normality testing across branches. two-group case (t-test vs Wilcoxon) multi-group case (ANOVA vs Kruskal-Wallis) now share normality test: shapiro.test() applied internally studentised residuals lm(y ~ x) via rstandard(). Variance homogeneity test changed bartlett.test() levene.test(). Brown-Forsythe Levene-type test (using median) robust non-normality Bartlett’s test. test now determines whether Student’s t-test (t.test(var.equal = TRUE)) Welch’s t-test used two-group case, whether aov() oneway.test() used multi-group case. Large-sample threshold changed 30 50 per group. every group exceeds 50 observations, normality testing skipped parametric tests applied directly (justified central limit theorem). Post-hoc test selection. oneway.test() (Welch’s ANOVA) used unequal variances, games.howell() appropriate post-hoc test, assume equal variances. TukeyHSD() remains aov() (Student’s ANOVA). Rank correlation (correlation = TRUE). Selects appropriate rank correlation data type: Spearman’s \(\rho\) two numeric variables, Kendall’s \(\tau_b\) variables ordered factors. test decision made automatically; requires explicit user choice.

title: visStatistics 0.2.0
text: Formula interface: visstat(y ~ x, data = df) now supported alongside existing visstat(x, y) visstat(dataframe, "namey", "namex") forms. Ordered factor responses: response class ordered, visstat() converts numeric ranks applies Wilcoxon Kruskal-Wallis. variables ordered correlation = TRUE, Kendall’s \(\tau_b\) used instead. Correlation analysis: New parameter correlation visstat(). set TRUE, selects Spearman rank correlation numeric variables Kendall’s \(\tau_b\) two ordered factors, instead fitting regression model. New exported functions: levene.test(): Brown-Forsythe Levene-type test homogeneity variance (center = median), mimicking default behaviour leveneTest() car package. bp.test(): Breusch-Pagan test heteroscedasticity linear regression models. games.howell(): Games-Howell post-hoc test pairwise comparisons following Welch’s ANOVA. vis_lm_assumptions(): Renamed vis_anova_assumptions(), now provides unified assumption diagnostics general linear model (t-test, ANOVA, regression).

title: visStatistics 0.2.0
text: vis_anova_assumptions() removed replaced vis_lm_assumptions(). new function handles ANOVA regression diagnostics (controlled regression parameter). regression, shows Residuals vs Leverage plot (Cook’s distance contours) Breusch-Pagan test instead Bartlett test.

title: visStatistics 0.2.0
text: Vignette substantially revised: Spearman correlation section rewritten, justification Spearman-approach added. Complete decision logic rewritten reflect new test selection algorithm. general linear model framework (Appendix ) introduced. Clarified rstandard() computes internally studentised residuals, reference Cook Weisberg (1982). Bibliography extended. DESCRIPTION rewritten reflect updated test selection algorithm.
